items.question: What must you do to the data before applying standard PCA?
options: Center each feature (subtract the column mean)
items.answer: Center each feature (subtract the column mean)
items.explanation: PCA directions maximize variance around the mean; centering aligns the geometry with the covariance matrix definition.

items.question: If the first two PCA components explain 0.40 cumulative variance on a 50-feature dataset, what does that imply?
options: Two components retain 40% of total variance; 60% remains in other directions
Two components retain 60% of total variance
The data has exactly two clusters
items.answer: Two components retain 40% of total variance; 60% remains in other directions
items.explanation: Cumulative explained variance ratio measures fraction of spread captured; low values suggest you may need more components for faithful compression.

items.question: Which implementation link is correct for centered data matrix X̃?
options: Principal directions are right singular vectors of SVD(X̃)
Principal directions are always the rows of U in SVD(X̃)
PCA requires X̃ to be square
Eigenvalues of X̃ equal explained variance directly
items.answer: Principal directions are right singular vectors of SVD(X̃)
items.explanation: X̃ = U Σ Vᵀ; columns of V (rows of Vᵀ) span principal axes; σᵢ² relates to variance along those axes.

items.question: You have age (small numeric range) and income (large numeric range). Which preprocessing is appropriate for balanced PCA?
options: Standardize features to unit variance before PCA
Drop income because its variance is larger
items.answer: Standardize features to unit variance before PCA
items.explanation: Without scaling, income dominates covariance PCA; standardizing yields correlation-style geometry with balanced influence.
